figures.gloss: The supreme being of the Khoekhoe — Tsui-//Goab, 'wounded knee,' who lives in the red dawn and rides the thunder-cloud, sending rain, granting health, and answering prayer at first light. He is the ancestral first-chief who warred against the black-sky lord //Gaunab, was struck on the knee in every battle, died, and rose again each time stronger, until he overcame death itself and became God.
figures.facet: A high god who is also a limping ancestor — the Khoekhoe make their sky-father a chief who DIED and rose repeatedly, his very name the scar of a wound. Where other supreme beings rule from an untouched heaven, Tsui-//Goab earns his godhead by being killed in battle again and again: divinity not as immunity but as the one who keeps coming back from the grave.
figures.provenance: Centrally documented in Theophilus Hahn, 'Tsuni-//Goam, the Supreme Being of the Khoi-Khoi' (1881); the etymology of the name ('wounded knee' vs. 'red dawn') was already contested in Hahn's day and is debated still, a contest the mirror keeps rather than resolves. Corroborated in the Bleek and Schapera ('The Khoisan Peoples of South Africa') records.

figures.gloss: The malevolent one — //Gaunab, who dwells in the black sky and the grave, the bringer of sickness, misfortune, and death. He is the eternal adversary of Tsui-//Goab, the one who wounded the god's knee in their endless war; from him come the ills the living suffer, and to his dark realm the dead are drawn. Where Tsui-//Goab is dawn and rain, //Gaunab is night and the pit.
figures.facet: An enemy-god who is not merely chaos but the principled author of death and disease — the Khoekhoe split the cosmos into a red-dawn benefactor and a black-sky destroyer locked in a war neither finally wins. The shard where southern Africa frames evil not as accident or ambivalence but as a named, opposed will: a genuine two-power cosmos.
figures.provenance: From Hahn (1881) and the 19th-c. Cape ethnography; later recorders and Schapera caution that missionary contact may have sharpened the good-versus-evil reading, so the dualism is flagged as possibly heightened in transmission, not invented by it.

figures.gloss: The great ancestor-hero and sorcerer of the Khoekhoe — Heitsi-Eibib, conceived by a virgin who ate a magical grass or was touched by sunlight, a shape-shifting trickster and prophet who taught the people, fought monsters, and died many deaths only to be found alive again over the next hill. His many graves — cairns of heaped stones — are scattered across the whole veld, for he died and rose so often that he lies buried everywhere at once.
figures.facet: An ancestor who has MANY graves because he rose from each one — virgin-born, serially killed, serially reborn, and never finally dead. The Khoekhoe scatter his tomb across the entire landscape: not one holy sepulchre but a country full of them, so that the dying-and-rising one is buried under every cairn the traveller passes.
figures.provenance: Among the best-attested Khoekhoe traditions — Hahn (1881), Bleek, and Schapera all record Heitsi-Eibib's virgin birth, repeated deaths and resurrections, and the cairn-graves; the heaping-of-stones rite was observed into the modern era, a living practice confirming the myth.

figures.gloss: The Moon sent the Hare to humankind with the words, 'As I die and am reborn, so shall you die and be reborn.' But the Hare garbled it, telling the people instead, 'As I die and perish utterly, so shall you perish.' For this lie death became final; and the Moon, in fury, struck the Hare across the mouth and split its lip — the cleft the hare carries to this day, the scar of the ruined message.
figures.facet: Death as a MESSENGER'S BLUNDER — the Moon, who visibly dies and is reborn each month, offered humankind that very rebirth on its own terms, and a careless Hare lost it for everyone by garbling the words. The Khoekhoe carve the error into the world: the split hare-lip is the permanent scar of the mistake, and our mortality is the cost of a message delivered wrong.
figures.provenance: A core Khoekhoe myth recorded by Hahn (1881) and Bleek; the moon-hare 'origin of death' recurs in close variant among the neighbouring San / |Xam and, in miscarried-message form, across much of sub-Saharan Africa — its regional density a datum in itself. The split-lip aetiology is the distinctively Khoe-San signature.

figures.gloss: The Sun — Sores — a hot and dangerous being for the Khoekhoe, who in one telling is a man whose armpit shines and is hauled into the sky by children so that all may share his light, or who must be thrown up each dawn. Less a throned god than a fierce power of heat and day, set against the cool benevolence of the dawn-god and the dying-rising Moon.
figures.facet: A sun that is a hazard rather than a king — the Khoekhoe rank the red DAWN and the dying-rising MOON above the blazing noon, making their solar figure a harsh, even unwelcome, power that must be managed. The shard where the sun is not the supreme light but a heat to be endured, eclipsed in honour by the gentler edges of the day.
figures.provenance: From Hahn (1881) and Khoe-San comparative ethnography (Schapera); the 'sun hauled up by children / shining armpit' motif is shared with San traditions, and the relatively low cultic rank of the Sun beside dawn and moon is noted across recorders.

figures.gloss: The rain — life itself to a desert-edge herding people — comes as the gift of Tsui-//Goab from the cloud-sky, the //Khaeb above. Prayed to at the red dawn, the rain-bringing thunder-cloud is the god's own working: the Khoekhoe address their supreme being precisely as the one 'who makes the clouds come' and waters the grazing on which the cattle, and so the people, live.
figures.facet: The sky and rain as the body of the dawn-god's benevolence — for the Khoekhoe the cloud is not a separate weather-power but the very medium through which Tsui-//Goab gives or withholds life. The shard where 'sky-father' collapses into 'rain-giver': in a parched land the proof of a good god is simply water on the grass.
figures.provenance: From Hahn (1881) and Schapera; the identification of Tsui-//Goab as rain-and-thunder god and the dawn-prayer for rain are well attested, though the //Khaeb is more a meteorological-cosmic locus than a fully personified deity, and is kept here as a force bound to the supreme being rather than a separate god.

figures.gloss: Wherever a cairn of Heitsi-Eibib lies along a path, the passing traveller adds a stone — and a green twig, a feather, or a muttered word — asking the dying-and-rising ancestor for strength, luck, and good hunting on the journey ahead. Each stone is a petition; the grave grows by the hands of every passer-by, so that the ancestor's many tombs swell, year on year, with the prayers of the living.
figures.facet: A grave you build a little higher every time you pass it — the Khoekhoe make veneration an act of the road, a stone laid for safe travel rather than a tended shrine or a spoken creed. The shard where the holy place is portable and cumulative: not one temple but a hundred wayside cairns, each raised grain by grain by the journeys of ordinary people.
figures.provenance: The stone-heaping at Heitsi-Eibib's cairns is an observed, long-living Khoekhoe practice recorded by Hahn (1881), Bleek, and Schapera, and reported by Cape travellers; among the most concretely attested elements of the tradition, rite and myth confirming each other on the ground.

edges.note: The axis of the whole Khoekhoe cosmos: the red-dawn benefactor and the black-sky destroyer locked in an endless war, //Gaunab wounding the god's knee in every battle. Same myth, opposite valence — light against dark, healing against death, named in one breath by Hahn.

Tsui-//Goab IS the rain-and-thunder god; the //Khaeb sky and the cloud's water are the medium of his benevolence, prayed to at the red dawn. The supreme being and the rain are one working, not two powers.

Two dying-and-rising figures of one people, sometimes conflated in the sources: the dawn-god who rose from death to become God, and the ancestor-hero who rose from many graves. The Khoekhoe tradition doubles the resurrection motif across god and ancestor.

The rite is the myth made ground: every cairn the traveller heaps higher is a grave of Heitsi-Eibib, the ancestor who died and rose so often he lies buried across the whole veld. Practice and story confirm each other stone by stone.

Within the Khoekhoe world the two answers to death stand opposed: the Hare's blunder made human death FINAL, while Heitsi-Eibib the ancestor keeps RISING from grave after grave — the rebirth humankind lost, embodied in the one ancestor who never finally dies.

The two great lights of the Khoekhoe sky, unequal in honour: the dying-and-rising Moon that offered humankind its rebirth, ranked above the harsh blazing Sun — the gentle cyclical light set against the hazardous noon.

The SAME regional moon-and-hare 'origin of death' shared by the neighbouring San / |Xam and the Khoekhoe — both Khoisan peoples of southern Africa, both telling of a moon's promise of return ruined by a hare and sealed with the split lip. A documented shared motif across adjacent Khoe-San traditions, not mere structural rhyme; attested as t2 by shared region and near-identical telling, though never a proven cognate.

The pan-African 'origin of death by miscarried message': the Khoekhoe Hare garbles the Moon's promise of rebirth as the Zulu lizard outruns the chameleon's word of life. The same shape recurring across unrelated southern-African families — the shared answer is the datum, never descent.

The Khoekhoe Hare's mistake is a near-archetypal instance of the joint itself: death entering the world not by sin but by a message delivered wrong, the lost gift of immortality preserved as a scar on the messenger.

Two dying-and-rising figures whose tombs multiply across the land — Heitsi-Eibib's scattered cairns echo the many shrines claiming a limb of the dismembered Osiris. Both make the holy dead present everywhere through a country full of graves, by wholly unrelated routes.

A loose touch: both are bright, beloved figures bound to death and the hope of return, but Baldr dies once and waits beyond Ragnarök while Heitsi-Eibib rises again and again within the world. The shared note is dying-and-rising; the structures diverge sharply. Flagged.

Two dying-and-rising sons of a miraculous conception — Heitsi-Eibib virgin-born of grass or sunlight, Dionysus twice-born — both shape-shifting wonder-workers repeatedly destroyed and renewed. Functional kin across unrelated families, never cognate.

Two trickster culture-heroes who teach humankind, fight monsters, and reshape the world by audacious cunning — the Khoekhoe ancestor and the Polynesian demigod both die in the end, but Heitsi-Eibib keeps rising where Maui is finally killed.

Two principled adversaries of order — the black-sky destroyer //Gaunab and the Hostile Spirit of Iran — each the named source of sickness, death, and disorder locked against a benevolent supreme will. A structural twin of opposed-power cosmologies, by independent routes; Khoekhoe dualism is flagged as possibly sharpened by missionary contact.

//Gaunab against Tsui-//Goab instantiates the two-co-eternal-powers joint — a red-dawn benefactor and a black-sky destroyer at endless war — one of southern Africa's clearest framings of evil as a named, opposed will rather than mere ambivalence.

Loose: both are dark lords of death and the underworld set against a bright sky-god, but Erlik sits within a layered shaman cosmos while //Gaunab is a battlefield adversary who wounds the god directly. Shared darkness, different machinery. Flagged.

Two storm-and-rain sky-powers whose godhead is bound up with combat — Tsui-//Goab warring eternally against //Gaunab as the Hittite weather-god battles the serpent Illuyanka. The supreme rain-bringer proven by his defeat-and-recovery in cosmic war.

Two thunder-and-rain high gods who give the storm and the water on which life depends; both ride the cloud and answer the prayer for rain. Functional kin of the storm-wielder office across unrelated families, never descent.

Neighbouring southern-African supreme beings who are also FIRST figures — but Unkulunkulu is the first ancestor who recedes into the reed-bed of the dead, while Tsui-//Goab is the first chief who rose from death to rule the dawn. First-ness diverging into withdrawal versus into resurrection, among adjacent peoples.

Loose: both personify the sun, but Surya is a radiant charioteer-god of high honour while Sores is a fierce heat ranked BELOW the dawn and moon — the solar joint shared, the valence inverted. Flagged to mark the divergence: the Khoekhoe sun is a hazard, not a king.

A reach: each wayside cairn is a small fixed point where the traveller touches the dying-rising ancestor for safe passage — a portable, cumulative holy place rather than a single world-pillar. Flagged: the joint fits only loosely, the Khoekhoe scattering the centre across the whole road.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Khoekhoe (Khoikhoi) — the Khoe-speaking pastoralist peoples of southwestern Africa, of whom the Nama of Namaqualand and Namibia are the principal surviving group. Its center is the dawn-and-rain sky-god Tsui-//Goab; the tradition was largely displaced by Christianity by about 1900 but survives in Nama oral memory and cultural revival.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos turns on a two-power opposition: the benevolent Tsui-//Goab of the red dawn and the rain against the malevolent //Gaunab of the black sky and the grave, locked in an endless war in which //Gaunab wounds the god's knee. Death became final through a garbled message — the Moon sent word of rebirth by the Hare, who told it wrong, and was struck on the lip for it. The dying-and-rising ancestor-hero Heitsi-Eibib, virgin-born and repeatedly killed and risen, lies buried under stone cairns scattered across the whole veld.
dossier.practice: Prayer was offered to Tsui-//Goab at the red dawn, chiefly for rain and health on which a herding people depended; travelers heaped a stone (with a twig or muttered word) on Heitsi-Eibib's wayside cairns for luck on the road. New-moon observances, rain-rites, and rites of passage rounded out a cult without temples or a professional priesthood, among cattle- and sheep-keeping communities.
dossier.canon: Wholly oral, preserved through 19th- and early-20th-century recorders: Theophilus Hahn's 'Tsuni-//Goam' (1881), Wilhelm Bleek's collections, and Isaac Schapera's synthesis.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds divinity earned through death — a supreme being who is also a limping ancestor, killed and risen again until he overcame death itself, godhead as return from the grave rather than untouched transcendence.
sources: Theophilus Hahn, 'Tsuni-//Goam: The Supreme Being of the Khoi-Khoi' (1881)
Isaac Schapera, 'The Khoisan Peoples of South Africa: Bushmen and Hottentots' (1930)
W.H.I. Bleek, 'Reynard the Fox in South Africa; or, Hottentot Fables and Tales' (1864)
